person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
*WHEN I COME BACK AROUND I'M GOING TO REPLACE ANYTHING YOU CHANGE BACK*
Ludwig II of Thuringia, nicknamed Louis the Iron (1128 – 14 October 1172 at Neuenburg Castle in Freyburg).
Life

He was born in 1128, the son of Louis I, who in 1131 became the first landgrave of Thuringia, and his wife, Hedwig of Gudensberg.[1] When Louis I died in 1140, King Conrad III of Germany enfeoffed the 12-year-old Louis II with the landgraviate. The Ludowingians had a good relationship with the Hohenstaufen, because Louis I had supported the election of Conrad III in 1138. The relationship was so good that it was arranged that Louis II should marry Judith, who was a niece of Conrad III and a half-sister of his successor Frederick Barbarossa. Until Conrad's death, Louis II stayed at the royal court, where he was given an education by the archbishop of Mainz and the bishop of Merseburg. He married Judith in 1150, and a year later his son and successor Louis III was born.

During Louis II's reign, the population of Thuringia was frequently bullied and harassed by the nobility. Louis began to intervene against these practises, earning him his nickname "Louis the Iron". According to a legend, which was recorded by Johannes Rothe in 1421, Louis II was travelling anonymously and one night, he found shelter with a blacksmith in Ruhla. The blacksmith, as he worked the iron in his forge, complained about the people's plight and cursed the nobility, lamenting that their lord was too soft towards them, and addressed the iron as if it were the landgrave himself: "Landgraf, werde hart!" (German: "Landgrave, become hard!" or "Landgrave, take a stance!") These words spurred Louis into action against the robber barons. According to the legend, after the offenders had been arrested, they were harnessed to a plough and forced to plough a field.

During his reign, Louis was allied with his brother-in-law Frederick Barbarossa, who became king in 1152 and was crowned emperor in 1155. They fought together against the Guelph Duke Henry the Lion and the archbishops of Mainz (who held Erfurt, among other possessions).

The Wartburg was further extended during Louis II's reign. The palace was constructed in its present form — radiocarbon dating shows that the oaks for the roof beams were felled in 1157. In 1168, he built Runneburg Castle in Weißensee and in 1184 he built Creuzburg Castle.

In 1170, Frederick and Louis undertook an expedition against Poland. After Louis's return, he fell ill and he died on 14 October 1172. Like almost all Thuringian landgraves, he was buried in the monastery in Reinhardsbrunn. Tomb stones for members of the family were transferred to the St. George church in Eisenach when the monastery was demolished.
Marriage and issue

In 1140, Louis II married Judith of Hohenstaufen, a half-sister of Emperor Frederick Barbarossa. They had the following children:

    Louis III "the Mild" (1151–1190),[2] his successor
    Henry Raspe III (c. 1155 – 18 July 1217), Count of Gudensberg[3]
    Frederick (c. 1155 – 1229), Count of Ziegenhain[4]
    Herman I (c. 1155 – 1217)[5]
    Judith, married Herman II, Count of Ravensberg[6]
    Sophia, married Bernhard, Count of Anhalt

zh.lifeSummary: 當Ludwig II. der Eiserne von Thüringen於1128年出生在德國賀林根藍德可萊斯 格塔福利德利可洛達萊音哈斯布盧恩時，他的父親Ludwig I. von Thüringen38歲，母親Hedwig von Gudensberg30歲。他於1150年與Jutta von Schwaben結婚。他們至少是3個兒子的父母。他去世於1172年10月14日在德國賀林根瓦特包可萊斯享年44歲，埋葬在德國賀林根。

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Thüringen
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German (Thüringer): habitational name for someone from Thuringia. The region is named from its former occupation by the T(h)uringii, an ancient Germanic tribe. Compare Deringer and Dieringer .

variants.name: Deringer
meanings: from a short form of an old personal name, a variant of Därr (see Darr ).
variant of Döringer, a habitational name for someone from Thuringia (see Doering ). Compare Derringer .

nameMeanings.name: Ludwig
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: from hlud ‘fame’ + wīg ‘war’; an ancient royal and imperial name. See Louis .

variants.name: Louis
meanings: name, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, from hlōd ‘fame’ + wīg ‘war’. It was very common in French royal and noble families. Louis I ( 778–840 ) was the son of Charlemagne, who ruled as both King of France and Holy Roman Emperor. Altogether, the name was borne by sixteen kings of France up to the French Revolution, in which Louis XVI perished. Louis XIV, ‘the Sun King’ ( 1638–1715 ), reigned for seventy-two years ( 1643–1715 ), presiding in the middle part of his reign over a period of unparalleled French power and prosperity. In modern times Louis is also found in the English-speaking world (usually pronounced ‘loo-ee’). In Britain the Anglicized form Lewis is rather more common, whereas in America the reverse is true.

nameMeanings.name: II.
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

nameMeanings.name: der
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of any of the given names beginning with the letter D- ( compare Jay and Kay ), in particular Dorothy . It is also used as an independent name, and may in some cases be associated with the River Dee ( compare Clyde ).

en.lifeSummary: When Ludwig II. der Eiserne von Thüringen was born in 1128, in Reinhardsbrunn, Friedrichroda, Gotha, Thuringia, Germany, his father, Ludwig I. von Thüringen, was 38 and his mother, Hedwig von Gudensberg, was 30. He married Jutta von Schwaben in 1150. They were the parents of at least 3 sons. He died on 14 October 1172, in Wartburgkreis, Thuringia, Germany, at the age of 44, and was buried in Thuringia, Germany.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Ludwig II. der Eiserne von Thüringen est né en 1128, à Reinhardsbrunn, Friedrichroda, Landkreis Gotha, Thüringen, Allemagne, son père, Ludwig I. von Thüringen, avait 38 ans et sa mère, Hedwig von Gudensberg, avait 30 ans. Il a épousé Jutta von Schwaben en 1150. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils. Il est décédé le 14 octobre 1172, à Wartburgkreis, Thüringen, Allemagne, à l’âge de 44 ans, et a été enterré à Thüringen, Allemagne.

ko.lifeSummary: Ludwig II. der Eiserne von Thüringen(이)가 1128년 독일 스u링 랜드크레이스 가스아 프리드리크로다 레인하르드스브루에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Ludwig I. von Thüringen(은)는 38세였고, 그의 어머니 Hedwig von Gudensberg(은)는 30세였다. 그는 1150년 Jutta von Schwaben(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1172년 10월 14일 독일 스u링 워트버그크레이스에서 44세의 나이로 사망했으며 독일 스u링에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Ludwig II. der Eiserne von Thüringen родился в 1128 г. в местности ринардсбрунн, фриедричрода, ландкрис гота, тuринген, Германия, его отцу по имени Ludwig I. von Thüringen было 38 лет, а матери, которую звали Hedwig von Gudensberg, 30. Он женился на женщине по имени Jutta von Schwaben в 1150 г. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына. Он умер 14 октября 1172 г. в местности вартбургкрис, тuринген, Германия в возрасте 44 лет и был похоронен в местности тuринген, Германия.
